target_audience.secondary: Those researching lab-grown as an alternative

writing_style.editorial_stance: The reader has chosen lab-grown diamonds — that decision is fully supported. The content exists to help them find the best lab-grown stone within their chosen shape and carat weight. Practical watch outs (e.g. the importance of certification, how grading can vary between labs) are framed as empowering knowledge, not concerns — and every watch out must be paired with a clear positive: what a well-chosen lab-grown stone delivers. Environmental context can be mentioned briefly as part of the lab-grown story, but only in passing — it should never dominate, never cite specific statistics, and should never be framed as a comparison against natural diamonds. Content should not read as anti-natural or advocacy. The site sells lab-grown diamonds and some affiliated retailers also sell natural diamonds. Content must be warm and informative, not polemical.
vocabulary_preference: the same physical and optical properties
what to look for in a well-graded stone
this is where lab-grown performs particularly well
avoid_words: identical to mined diamonds at the atomic level

prompt_rules: Never position lab-grown diamonds against natural diamonds — present lab-grown as its own category with its own strengths.
Frame engagement ring guidance around the freedom that lab-grown pricing offers: larger stones, better cuts, or savings redirected to setting and design.
Emphasise transparency and traceability as positive qualities in their own right, not as criticisms of alternatives.
content_angles: Focus on how lab-grown pricing at this carat weight opens up options that would be budget-prohibitive in natural — what becomes possible.
Focus on quality consistency in lab-grown stones at this combination — how controlled growth conditions affect what buyers can expect.
Focus on choosing a setting that celebrates the stone's origin story — modern designs that complement a conscious purchase.
Focus on how this carat weight in lab-grown compares to what buyers typically consider — where it sits in the range of popular engagement ring choices.
Focus on the grading and certification landscape for lab-grown at this size — what to look for and what reports mean.

quality_comparison_static.instruction: How to identify a great lab-grown {carat}-carat {shape} diamond. Focus on what certification to look for (IGI, GCAL) and why grading rigour matters for lab-grown stones. Cover the key quality factors at this carat weight and what a well-certified stone actually delivers in terms of appearance. Frame entirely as empowering knowledge — what to look for to get a beautiful stone. Do not draw comparisons to mined diamonds.

buying_guidance_static.instruction: Practical buying advice for lab-grown {carat}-carat {shape} diamonds. Cover which quality factors matter most at this size, what certification to prioritise and why, and where buyers can be selective to maximise their choice. Every watch out must be immediately paired with a concrete positive about what a well-chosen lab-grown stone at this size delivers.

faq_static.instruction: Write for a buyer who has chosen lab-grown and wants practical knowledge to buy confidently. Generate questions specific to lab-grown {carat}-carat {shape} diamonds — certification questions, quality questions, and practical buying questions this audience genuinely has. Focus on empowering the buyer, not on comparisons to mined diamonds. Examples: "Does IGI grade lab-grown {shape} diamonds the same way as natural ones?", "What certification should I look for in a lab-grown {carat}-carat {shape}?", "Is there a quality difference between HPHT and CVD lab-grown {shape} diamonds at {carat} carats?". Do not draw comparisons to mined diamonds or reference mining. Answers should be factual, clear, and empowering — helping the buyer feel confident. Include lab-grown search forms: price, cost, how much, worth. No years or today.

carat_sites.brand_positioning: Your complete guide to {carat} carat diamond rings
carat_sites.tagline: What a {carat} carat diamond ring costs, looks like, and how to choose one

target_audience.primary: Buyers shopping for a {carat} carat diamond ring
target_audience.secondary: Those researching {carat} carat diamonds before buying a ring

writing_style.editorial_stance: The reader is shopping for a diamond ring at this carat weight. They arrived by searching "{carat} carat diamond ring" — the finished ring is the product, not just the loose stone. Content should cover the complete ring: loose diamond pricing (our core data), setting styles and their costs, total ring budget, and what the ring looks like on a hand. The carat decision is fully supported — never suggest they reconsider it. At larger sizes, setting choice becomes especially important; at smaller sizes, cut quality and shape choice have the biggest visual impact. Every section should help the reader go from "I want a {carat} carat diamond ring" to "I know exactly what to look for and what to pay." The tone is clear, helpful, and celebratory.
vocabulary_preference: the diamond itself costs… and the setting adds…
what to look for at this size
how it looks on a hand
the setting becomes especially important at this size
what makes a great {carat}-carat diamond ring

all_shapes.intro_text:

Depending on shape, {color_word}, and clarity, the stone alone ranges from {currency_symbol}{min_price:,.0f} to {currency_symbol}{max_price:,.0f} — worth pinning down before you add the setting to build your full ring budget.
all_shapes.shape_analysis:
For a {carat} carat ring, the {most_expensive_shape} is the most expensive centre stone at {currency_symbol}{most_expensive_price:,.0f}, while the {least_expensive_shape} is the most budget-friendly at {currency_symbol}{least_expensive_price:,.0f} — about {currency_symbol}{price_difference:,.0f} ({price_difference_pct:.1f}%) that could go toward a better setting or a higher cut grade.
We track {total_sample_size:,} {type} stones across {shape_count} shapes; the weighted median centre-stone price is {currency_symbol}{weighted_median_price:,.0f}.

prompt_rules: Always write about the complete ring, not just the loose diamond — settings, bands, and total cost matter as much as the stone.
This audience is buying a ring for a proposal or milestone. Frame every point as helping them choose confidently and celebrate the moment.
Avoid industry jargon without explanation — if you mention a technical term, immediately follow with what it means for how the ring looks on the hand.
content_angles: Focus on total ring cost at this carat weight — how much the setting adds, what band styles cost, and where the total typically lands.
Focus on how this carat weight looks on different hand sizes — whether it feels statement or subtle and what setting amplifies presence.
Focus on daily wearability at this carat weight — durability of settings, snag risk, and how to protect the stone during everyday life.
Focus on what makes this carat weight a common choice vs stepping up or down — the visual difference and price difference per step.
Focus on proposal-ready combinations: ring styles that photograph well, look impressive at a glance, and stay comfortable for years.
Focus on the most common mistake buyers make at this carat weight — overspending on one factor while under-investing in another that matters more.

on_hand_appearance_static.instruction: Write clearly for a beginner buyer choosing a {carat}-carat {shape} diamond ring. Describe what this shape realistically looks like on a hand at {carat} carats: face-up size impression, how the proportions interact with finger width, and whether the shape appears larger or smaller than its carat weight suggests. Be specific to this shape — the length-to-width ratio for fancy shapes, the spread for rounds, the visual weight distribution. This is the context a buyer needs to picture the stone on their hand before committing. Frame positively: what makes a {carat}-carat {shape} look impressive on the hand, not what its limitations are.

setting_guide_static.instruction: Write clearly for a beginner buyer choosing a setting for a {carat}-carat {shape} diamond ring. Cover the setting styles that work best for this shape at this size — which settings complement the stone's proportions, which offer practical protection for daily wear at this carat weight, and roughly how setting choice affects total ring cost. At larger carat weights, setting security becomes especially important; at smaller weights, the setting's contribution to overall visual size matters more. Give 2-3 specific setting recommendations with a one-line reason for each. Do not recommend against any setting categorically — frame as trade-offs.

buying_guidance_static.instruction: Write clearly for a beginner buyer purchasing a {carat}-carat {shape} diamond ring. Give practical guidance on which quality factors to prioritise at this carat weight and shape: where to focus budget (cut vs colour vs clarity), what is visible to the naked eye at this size, and where a buyer can save without compromising how the ring looks in everyday wear. Every recommendation should consider the complete ring — not just the loose stone. Lead with the single most impactful piece of advice for someone buying a ring at this specific combination. Frame as confident, celebratory guidance — this is an exciting purchase and the content should help, not overwhelm.

faq_static.instruction: Write for a beginner buyer researching {carat}-carat {shape} diamond rings. Generate questions that someone shopping for a ring at this carat weight would actually ask — about total ring cost, how the diamond looks in a setting, quality trade-offs, and practical concerns about wearing a ring at this size daily. Examples of the right register: "How much does a {carat} carat {shape} diamond ring cost in total?", "What setting is best for a {carat}-carat {shape}?", "Can you tell the difference between VS1 and VS2 at {carat} carats?", "Is a {carat}-carat {shape} too big for everyday wear?". Answers must be concise, helpful, and ring-focused — not just about the loose stone. At least one question must address total ring budget (stone + setting). Vary ring, price, cost, how much, on hand, and loose-diamond phrasing. No years or today.

query_phrase_emphasis: how much does a {carat} carat {shape} ring cost
average cost of {carat} carat diamond ring

comparison.methodology_note: Ring price comparisons use the median loose diamond price. Sources differ in which retailers and quality grades (colour, clarity) they include — a narrower quality range typically reports higher prices.

diamond_price_exact_sites.brand_positioning: The definitive local diamond pricing reference

target_audience.primary: Diamond buyers who want to know what they should pay
target_audience.secondary: Researchers, appraisers, and professionals needing current market pricing

writing_style.editorial_stance: The reader wants to know what a diamond costs. Give them the answer quickly and clearly, then provide the context they need to understand what drives that price. Do not tell them what to buy or what is "worth it" — present the pricing data and let them decide. When explaining price differences between shapes, carat weights, or quality grades, frame them as factual observations, not recommendations. The pricing data is a tool — the reader is in control.
vocabulary_preference: prices range from X to Y
at this carat weight, expect to pay
the price difference between X and Y is typically
price varies by approximately X% between
here is how the price breaks down
the most common price point is

prompt_rules: This is a pricing reference site — prioritise clarity and directness over personality. Every sentence should help the reader understand what they will pay.
Frame quality guidance through the lens of an engagement ring purchase — what looks good on the hand, not what scores well on a grading report.
Always acknowledge the local market context: currency, typical price range in that country, and how local prices compare to global benchmarks.
content_angles: Focus on what drives the price at this carat weight in this country — the dominant factors that move the number up or down.
Focus on the quality grades that represent best value for engagement ring buyers in this market — where most local buyers land and why.
Focus on how shape choice affects price at this carat weight — which shapes cost more locally and what buyers get in return.
Focus on the price range buyers should expect — the gap between entry-level and premium at this combination, and what separates them.
Focus on timing and market conditions — whether prices at this combination are stable, trending, or volatile and what that means for buying now.

pricing_context_static.instruction: Factual pricing context for {carat}-carat {shape} diamonds. Describe the general price range buyers encounter at this carat weight and shape, what factors cause the most variation in price (cut quality, colour, clarity), and where the steepest price jumps occur. Do not recommend specific grades or trade-offs — present the pricing landscape as factual observations that help the reader interpret the data. Ground all claims in general market patterns; this is static content without live data.

faq_static.instruction: Write as a neutral pricing reference. Generate questions that someone checking diamond prices would ask — questions about what drives the price, how prices differ by spec, and what to expect at this carat weight and shape. Answers should be factual and grounded in general market patterns — state what the data shows, do not advise. Use neutral price/cost/how much phrasing. No calendar years or today in static copy.

shape_preview.instruction: Factual overview of how diamond shape affects price in {country_name}. Name the shapes buyers search most often at common carat weights and note typical price ordering (e.g. round vs fancy shapes) without recommending a purchase. Neutral reference tone — the reader is comparing options before drilling into a specific carat or shape page.

price_factors_static.instruction: Explain what drives diamond prices in {country_name}: carat weight, cut, colour, clarity, shape, and natural vs lab-grown. State how the site's index is built (multiple online retailers, daily refresh, median prices from listed inventory). Do not recommend grades — present methodology so the reader trusts the numbers on this page.

target_audience.secondary: Those wanting reassurance in their diamond purchase

writing_style.editorial_stance: The reader has chosen a shape or is close to choosing one. Our role is to help them buy the best version of what they want — with confidence. We are their advisor, not a gatekeeper. Recommendations should reinforce their choice and equip them with the knowledge to select well. The "cautions" section exists to protect buyers from common mistakes, not to cast doubt on their decision: every caution must be paired with a clear positive — what to look for, not just what to avoid. Alternatives to the chosen shape should only appear as contextual comparisons, never as a suggestion to reconsider.
vocabulary_preference: where we see buyers do well
the quality standard we hold to
this is where the choice pays off
what to look for at this carat weight

prompt_rules: Write as a senior advisor guiding a client through a considered purchase. Recommendations should feel bespoke, not generic.
Frame engagement ring decisions as investments in craftsmanship and personal meaning — not financial investments, but choices worth making well.
When presenting trade-offs, always lead with the positive outcome of the recommended path before explaining what was traded away.
content_angles: Focus on what separates an exceptional stone from a merely good one at this carat weight — the subtle quality indicators that connoisseurs notice.
Focus on how setting and ring design choices can elevate this carat weight — making a considered stone look its absolute best.
Focus on the due diligence a discerning buyer should perform at this price point — what to verify, what to compare, and when to walk away.
Focus on provenance and certification at this carat weight — which labs are most trusted and why certification matters more at higher values.
Focus on long-term satisfaction: how this combination will look after years of wear, and what choices today ensure enduring beauty.
Focus on the relationship between cut precision and light performance at this shape and size — where marginal differences yield dramatic visual results.
Focus on what to pay for a {carat}-carat {shape} — price, cost, and worth in buyer terms.
Focus on engagement ring decisions: {carat}-carat centre stone plus setting trade-offs.

recommendations_static.instruction: Clear, confident recommendations for buyers choosing {carat}-carat {shape} diamonds. Lead with what to prioritise and explain the reasoning plainly. Tone: authoritative but warm — a trusted advisor speaking directly, not a rulebook. Support the reader's choice; recommendations exist to help them buy the best version of what they want.

quality_standards_static.instruction: The quality standards that matter most at this shape and carat weight. What grades or proportion ranges should a discerning buyer look for, and why? Frame as practical thresholds a buyer can act on — what is worth paying more for, and what represents genuine quality at this size. Explain the reasoning behind each standard in accessible terms.

cautions_static.instruction: What discerning buyers should be aware of when choosing {carat}-carat {shape} diamonds. Frame each caution as protective knowledge that helps them buy with confidence — not as a reason to doubt their choice. Every caution must be immediately followed by what to look for instead, giving the buyer a clear positive action. Tone: reassuring and empowering, not cautionary.

faq_static.instruction: Write as an experienced, trusted diamond advisor. Generate questions that a discerning buyer researching a {carat}-carat {shape} diamond would ask — questions about quality thresholds, certification, proportion standards, and what separates a genuinely good stone from an exceptional one at this size. Reflect an intermediate buyer who wants to buy well, not just buy cheap: "What cut grade should I insist on for a {carat}-carat {shape}?", "Is there a meaningful visual difference between VS1 and VS2 in a {carat}-carat {shape}?", "What proportion range gives the best face-up appearance in a {shape} at {carat} carats?". Answers should be warm but authoritative — clear recommendations with plain reasoning, as an experienced advisor would give a valued client. Vary Ahrefs-style forms: price, cost, worth, how much. No calendar years or today.

writing_style.editorial_stance: The reader has chosen a shape or is close to choosing one. The job is to help them find the best stone within that choice — not to nudge them elsewhere. British understatement is welcome; negativity without a follow-up is not. The tone is a knowledgeable friend who has done the legwork for UK buyers — familiar, direct, and on their side.

faq_static.instruction: Write as a value-focused, data-grounded guide. Generate questions that a budget-conscious buyer researching a {carat}-carat {shape} diamond would ask — questions about where the price is and is not justified, how to get the best stone for the money, and which quality trade-offs are genuinely worth making. Focus on the pricing reality specific to this shape and carat weight: "Where does the price premium go on a {carat}-carat {shape}?", "What color and clarity combination gives the best value at {carat} carats in a {shape}?", "Is it worth paying for GIA certification on a {carat}-carat {shape}?". Answers should be direct and grounded in general market patterns — no live prices, but confident pattern-based guidance. Vary question forms: worth, value, cost, how much, and price. No calendar years or words like today or currently.

query_phrase_emphasis: is it worth paying for at {carat} carats
lab-grown vs natural value at this weight

differentiator.focus: Best value hunting - is this a good deal?

graph_style.meta_description_format: How much is a {carat} carat {shape} {type} diamond worth in {country}? Value and price analysis across {sample_size} listings — chart updated {analysis_date}.

all_shapes.intro_text:
Right now, a {carat} carat {type_label} diamond {in_country} is going for about {currency_symbol}{current_price:,.0f}, based on the {num_diamonds:,} stones we're tracking at this weight.
They run from {currency_symbol}{min_price:,.0f} all the way up to {currency_symbol}{max_price:,.0f}, so where you land depends on shape, {color_word}, and clarity — and that's exactly where the value is hiding.
all_shapes.shape_analysis:
The best-value shape here is the {least_expensive_shape} at {currency_symbol}{least_expensive_price:,.0f}, while the {most_expensive_shape} is the priciest at {currency_symbol}{most_expensive_price:,.0f} — switching saves about {currency_symbol}{price_difference:,.0f} ({price_difference_pct:.1f}%).
We're tracking {total_sample_size:,} {type} stones across {shape_count} shapes; the typical price is {currency_symbol}{weighted_median_price:,.0f}.

comparison.methodology_note: We use the average (mean) price across a wide retailer pool. Other sources may use median pricing or different quality filters — minor differences reflect methodology, not errors.

target_audience.secondary: Those researching diamond prices in their region

writing_style.editorial_stance: The reader has chosen a shape or is close to choosing one. Content exists to help them find the best stone within that choice — with practical guidance relevant to their local market. Watch outs (e.g. what to prioritise at a given carat weight, where quality trade-offs are visible) should be paired with a concrete positive: what a well-chosen stone at this shape and size delivers. The tone is that of a knowledgeable local guide — professional and helpful, without the personal voice of an individual expert. Local market context (currency, retailers, regional pricing patterns) can be woven in naturally where it adds value, but the core diamond guidance is universal.
vocabulary_preference: what to look for at this carat weight
where quality matters most at this size
the priority at this shape and size is
what makes a strong choice at this carat weight

graph_style.meta_description_format: Current {carat} carat {shape} {type} diamond prices in {country} — distribution across {sample_size} diamonds. Price chart {analysis_date}.

all_shapes.intro_text:
A {carat} carat {type_label} diamond in {country_name} currently sits at {currency_symbol}{current_price:,.0f}, based on the {num_diamonds:,} stones we track at this weight{intro_change_clause}.
Across the shapes, prices run from {currency_symbol}{min_price:,.0f} to {currency_symbol}{max_price:,.0f} — what you pay comes down mostly to shape, {color_word}, and clarity once the stone is set in a ring.
all_shapes.shape_analysis: At {carat} carats, {most_expensive_shape} leads on price at {currency_symbol}{most_expensive_price:,.0f} while {least_expensive_shape} is the most accessible at {currency_symbol}{least_expensive_price:,.0f} — a {currency_symbol}{price_difference:,.0f} gap ({price_difference_pct:.1f}%).

diamond_type.intro:
{type_title} diamond prices in {country_name} span {carat_count} carat weights in our index — from entry-level sizes to stones well above the 1 carat mark.
{most_popular_carat} carat is the most searched weight, with a median of {currency_symbol}{weighted_median_price:,.0f} across every shape at that weight.
diamond_type.buyer_context: This page shows how {type_title_lower} diamond prices scale by carat weight in {country_name}. Each figure is a weighted median across every shape in our index — use it to set a budget before you compare round, oval, and other shapes at the weight you have in mind.

top_level.intro:
Our index tracks {total_diamonds_tracked:,} diamonds in {country_name} — {natural_sample_size:,} natural stones and {lab_sample_size:,} lab-grown across every shape and carat weight we monitor.
At 1 carat, the typical natural diamond sits at {currency_symbol}{one_carat_natural:,.0f} versus {currency_symbol}{one_carat_lab:,.0f} for lab-grown — a {one_carat_discount:.0f}% price gap that generally widens at larger weights.
top_level.market_size: Use the charts and table below to compare natural and lab-grown prices by carat weight and explore any size in more detail. {trend_clause}

prompt_rules: Write as though explaining to a friend who has just started ring shopping — knowledgeable and warm, never condescending.
Always contextualise prices and quality grades for engagement ring buyers — the diamond will be set in a ring and worn every day.
Prefer local framing where possible: reference the buying experience in the reader's country rather than generic global advice.
content_angles: Focus on what makes this shape popular for engagement rings in this market — why buyers choose it and what they should expect to pay.
Focus on quality trade-offs that matter most when the stone is set — which imperfections disappear once mounted and which remain visible.
Focus on how this carat weight sits on the hand in a ring setting — visual coverage, proportion, and how it compares to what most local buyers choose.
Focus on the buying journey: what to look for first, what to compare, and when to stop upgrading quality grades.
Focus on how local pricing for this shape compares to the global average — whether local buyers get better or worse value and why.

shape_breakdown_static.instruction: Write as a knowledgeable local jewellery guide. Compare diamond shapes at {carat} carats for {diamond_type} diamonds for engagement ring buyers: face-up size on the hand, sparkle character, durability in daily wear, and typical trade-offs at this weight. Ground every point in practical ring-shopping context — not abstract gemology. No specific prices or market movements. Separate paragraphs with a blank line (two newlines between each paragraph).

faq_static.instruction: Write as a knowledgeable local jewellery guide — professional, clear, and helpful. Generate questions a buyer comparing shapes at {carat} carats for {diamond_type} engagement rings would realistically ask: face-up size on the hand, which shapes offer the best value at this weight, durability in daily wear, and where to start once they have chosen a carat. Answers must be concise and practical — no hedging, no live prices or market commentary.

Write for buyers on a {diamond_type} diamond prices page. Questions should help them use the carat ladder, set a budget before comparing shapes, and understand when natural versus lab-grown matters for their ring — warm local-jeweller tone, engagement-ring focus. No today's prices, percentages, or market moves.

natural_vs_lab_analysis.instruction: Write for buyers on a {diamond_type} diamond prices page. Explain how natural and lab-grown stones compare on grading, value perceptions, and when each type suits an engagement ring — warm, local-jeweller tone. No today's prices or market moves. Separate paragraphs with a blank line (two newlines between each paragraph).

faq_static.instruction: Write for visitors landing on the diamond price hub. Questions should orient first-time buyers: natural versus lab-grown, how to read the charts and carat table, where to go next for a specific carat or shape, and what to decide before visiting a jeweller — practical engagement-ring guidance in a warm local-jeweller voice. No price figures, percentages, or dates.

type_comparison.instruction: Orient first-time visitors to natural versus lab-grown diamond prices on this site. Explain what each section of the hub shows and how to choose which type to explore first for an engagement ring — practical, non-salesy. No price figures or dates. Separate paragraphs with a blank line (two newlines between each paragraph).

shape_preview.instruction: Explain how diamond shape changes the look of a stone at the same carat weight and why buyers compare shapes before locking a budget. Encourage drilling into shape and carat pages from this hub. No specific prices or market commentary. Separate paragraphs with a blank line (two newlines between each paragraph).

price_factors_static.instruction: Write a substantial, genuinely useful explainer on what drives diamond prices for an engagement-ring buyer in {country}. Cover the structural factors: the 4Cs (cut, colour, clarity, carat) and how each moves price, natural versus lab-grown supply and demand, and the role of certification. Then bring it local — explain how factors specific to {country} (import duties or taxes, currency, retail margins, and what buyers there tend to prioritise) shape what shoppers actually pay, so the advice is rooted in the {country} buying experience rather than generic global commentary. Keep the warm, knowledgeable local-jeweller voice. No specific prices, figures, or dates — the live data on this hub handles current numbers. Separate paragraphs with a blank line (two newlines between each paragraph).

trend_analysis_static.instruction: Write as a knowledgeable local jewellery guide — professional, helpful, and grounded. Explain the structural pricing factors for {carat}-carat {shape} diamonds: what drives prices at this weight and shape combination, why certain quality grades carry premiums, and where the market typically settles for stones in this range. This is durable context that helps the reader understand the price graph above — not commentary on today's prices. Frame every factor as useful knowledge for a confident purchase. Do not quote specific prices or market movements — the graph handles that. Separate paragraphs with a blank line (two newlines between each paragraph).

buying_guidance_static.instruction: Write as a knowledgeable local jewellery guide — professional, helpful, neutral. Give practical guidance for buying a {carat}-carat {shape} diamond: which quality factors to prioritise at this size (cut, colour, clarity), where a buyer can be selective without sacrificing visible quality, and what to watch for that is specific to this shape at {carat} carats. Every watch-out must be paired with a concrete positive action. Lead with the most impactful recommendation for this particular combination. Do not second-guess the reader's shape choice. Separate paragraphs with a blank line (two newlines between each paragraph).

faq_static.instruction: Write as a knowledgeable local jewellery guide — professional, clear, and helpful. Generate questions that a buyer researching a {carat}-carat {shape} diamond would realistically search for or ask in a jewellery store — not generic diamond questions but practical doubts, trade-offs, and value questions specific to this shape and carat weight. Examples of the right register: "What colour grade is good enough for a {carat}-carat {shape}?", "Does clarity matter more at {carat} carats for a {shape}?", "Where is the best value at {carat} carats for a {shape}?". Answers must be concise, helpful, and grounded in general diamond pricing patterns — no hedging, no live prices. At least one question must address a common quality trade-off specific to this shape at {carat} carats. Vary price, cost, worth, how much, and ring phrasing. No years or today in static copy.

query_phrase_emphasis: how much does a {shape} cost at {carat} carats

comparison_labels.intro: {carat} carat {shape} {type} diamond prices — how local pricing compares

comparison.methodology_note: Each price source tracks a different mix of local and international retailers. Differences in which colour and clarity grades are included also affect the reported price.

market_intelligence.brand_positioning: Automated retail diamond market intelligence for upstream industry
market_intelligence.tagline: What moved the market — and why

target_audience.secondary: Funds, insurers, and trade bodies with diamond exposure

report_charts.title: Lab vs natural price gap by carat band

sections.title: Standing Market Conditions
sections.description: Ongoing factors (sanctions, tariffs, policy) carried from prior months

sections.title: Industry Context
sections.description: How this month's signals align with or contrast published industry outlooks (De Beers, Bain, etc.)

editorial_rules: Correlation language only — never 'caused by' or 'due to' for macro links
Every figure must appear in the Analyst findings JSON
Month label in the cover headline and executive summary opening
Self-contained paragraphs — no 'see chart above' or 'click to explore'
Standing conditions use present tense while lifecycle is ongoing
Chart captions state the takeaway in one sentence
Industry context cites named sources only from industry_reference catalogue — paraphrase themes, never reproduce copyrighted report text
Industry alignment language: 'consistent with', 'contrasts with', 'extends' — not 'proves' or 'confirms'

ringspo.brand_positioning: Independent diamond guidance from someone who knows the market and talks plainly
ringspo.tagline: Expert advice without the jewellery-store sales pitch

writing_style.editorial_stance: The reader has already made or is close to making their choice. The default tone is supportive of that decision — they are not being talked out of anything. Trade-offs and things to be aware of should be framed as practical guidance that helps them buy the best version of what they want, not as reasons to reconsider. Alternatives to the current page's shape or size should only be mentioned in passing as context, never as a recommendation to go elsewhere.
vocabulary_preference: where the extra spend is not doing much for you

voice_examples.direct_recommendation: "My recommendation for color is H — it looks white, but costs significantly less than D or E for a difference you genuinely cannot see face-up."
voice_examples.buyer_protective_plain: "Jewelers push higher clarity than you need because it's easy to demonstrate under a microscope. Don't fall for it — VS2 is the sweet spot."
voice_examples.plain_why: "Cut matters more than anything else here. A well-cut stone hides color and clarity differences that would otherwise cost you real money to fix."
voice_examples.blunt_tradeoff: "Yes, rounds cost more than ovals or cushions of the same weight and quality. That's just what the market does. The upside is there are far more of them to compare, so you have more leverage when shopping."
voice_examples.note: The examples above show voice and register — direct, opinionated, buyer-side, plainspoken. Use this register throughout. Do not copy these sentences verbatim into output. Generate your own specific content in this voice.

prompt_rules: Frame every recommendation through the lens of an engagement ring purchase — the diamond will be set, worn daily, and seen by others.
When discussing cut quality, always explain the real-world impact on finger sparkle at arm's length, not just grading lab metrics.
Prefer concrete price-behaviour observations over abstract market commentary — e.g. 'H colour saves you 20-30% vs D with no visible difference' rather than 'colour grades affect price'.
Mirror Ahrefs-style search phrasing in FAQs (price, worth, cost, how much, ring) without repeating the same head term in every sentence.
content_angles: Focus on how this diamond looks once set in a ring — finger coverage, sparkle at normal viewing distance, and how the setting affects perceived size.
Focus on where the smart money sits at this carat weight — which quality grades deliver the best visual result per dollar spent.
Focus on the trade-offs a first-time buyer faces at this carat weight — what to prioritise and what to relax on without regret.
Focus on how cut quality interacts with this shape at this carat weight — what separates a lively stone from a flat one.
Focus on practical ring-building: how setting style, band width, and side stones interact with this centre stone's proportions.
Focus on comparing this carat weight to adjacent weights — where the sweet spots and price jumps are.
Focus on how much a {carat}-carat {shape} is worth versus what buyers actually pay — worth, value, and price in plain language.
Focus on {carat}-carat diamond ring budget — ring price, centre stone price, and what the total purchase looks like.
Focus on loose diamond vs ring framing — what changes when the same {carat}-carat {shape} is purchased as a loose stone versus set.

expert_take_static.instruction: Write in the Ringspo voice: direct, opinionated, plainspoken, buyer-side. Lead with the single most useful thing someone buying a {carat}-carat {shape} needs to hear — not an observation about the market, but a practical opinion that shapes how they should approach this purchase. Sound like a knowledgeable friend giving honest advice, not an analyst summarising a category. Skip preambles. No market commentary as an opener. Get straight to what matters for this specific shape and size.

pricing_context_static.instruction: Write in the Ringspo voice: plain, direct, matter-of-fact. Explain the structural reasons why {carat}-carat {shape} diamonds are priced the way they are — the manufacturing, cutting, or demand factors that are built into the price regardless of market conditions. This is durable context that the price graph cannot show. Keep it positive and practical: frame each pricing factor as useful knowledge that helps the buyer understand what they are paying for and shop confidently. Do not comment on price levels or market movements — the graph handles that. Do not mention resale or investment value.

buying_guidance_static.instruction: Write in the Ringspo voice: blunt recommendations, clear priorities, plain explanations of why. Give specific guidance on cut grade, color, and clarity for {carat}-carat {shape} diamonds — what to prioritise, where to save without losing visible quality, and the most common mistake buyers make here. Every watch-out must be immediately paired with a clear positive action. Sound like someone who has seen a lot of buyers make avoidable mistakes and wants to help this buyer avoid them. Lead with the most important recommendation, not with a structure of topics.

faq_static.instruction: Write in the Ringspo voice: direct, no-nonsense, buyer-protective. Generate questions that a real person shopping for a {carat}-carat {shape} diamond would actually type into Google or ask a knowledgeable friend — not generic diamond questions, but doubts, trade-offs, and money questions specific to this shape and carat weight. Examples of the right register: "Do I really need VVS for a {carat}-carat {shape}?", "Why does a {shape} cost what it does at {carat} carats?", "Is H colour good enough for a {carat}-carat {shape}?". Answers must be short, direct, and opinionated — no hedging, no preamble. Sound like a knowledgeable friend giving the honest answer, not a retailer trying to upsell. At least one question must address a common money mistake specific to this shape at {carat} carats. Use varied Ahrefs-style question forms across the three FAQs: at least one with "how much" or "worth", one with "price" or "cost", and one about the {carat}-carat {shape} in a ring or on the hand. Never ask which website or retailer to buy from — that is retailer-guide copy, not Ringspo. Never ask generic "compare to other shapes" questions Loupe-style; stay on this shape and carat. Never include calendar years or words like today or currently.

query_phrase_emphasis: loose diamond vs engagement ring budget

graph_style.meta_description_format: Independent market analysis of {carat} carat {shape} {type} diamond prices in {country} — what buyers pay across {sample_size} diamonds. Price chart and distribution updated {analysis_date}.

all_shapes.intro_text:
Right now a {carat} carat {type_label} diamond {in_country} runs {currency_symbol}{current_price:,.0f} — that's the median across the {num_diamonds:,} stones we track at this weight{intro_change_clause}.
Expect anywhere from {currency_symbol}{min_price:,.0f} to {currency_symbol}{max_price:,.0f} depending on shape, {color_word}, and clarity. The spread is wide, which means there's real room to shop smart.
all_shapes.shape_analysis:
The {most_expensive_shape} is the most expensive cut at this weight ({currency_symbol}{most_expensive_price:,.0f}); the {least_expensive_shape} is the cheapest ({currency_symbol}{least_expensive_price:,.0f}) — roughly a {price_difference_pct:.1f}% premium ({currency_symbol}{price_difference:,.0f}).
If you're flexible on shape, that's money better spent on a higher cut grade. We track {total_sample_size:,} {type} stones across {shape_count} shapes; the weighted median is {currency_symbol}{weighted_median_price:,.0f}.

comparison_labels.intro: How {carat} carat {shape} {type} diamond prices compare across independent sources

comparison.methodology_note: Prices reflect the median across major online retailers. Each source uses a slightly different retailer mix and inclusion criteria, so small differences are expected.

shape_breakdown_static.instruction: Write expert comparison of diamond shapes at {carat} carats for {diamond_type} diamonds. Explain how shapes differ in face-up size, sparkle character, and typical buyer trade-offs at this weight — without quoting specific prices or daily market moves.

expert_recommendations.instruction: Give site-voice recommendations for choosing a shape at {carat} carats. Be opinionated and practical: who each shape suits, what to prioritise, and one mistake to avoid. No price figures or market commentary.

natural_vs_lab_analysis.instruction: Write an in-depth but accessible comparison of natural versus lab-grown diamonds for buyers researching {diamond_type} options. Cover grading, resale perceptions, environmental narratives, and where each type makes sense — without citing today's prices.

buying_considerations.instruction: Explain what buyers should weigh when choosing {diamond_type} diamonds — certification, cut quality, where to be selective, and common pitfalls. Practical guidance only; no specific dollar amounts.

market_guidance_static.instruction: Write broad market-level guidance for someone landing on the main diamond prices hub. Explain how to use the site's data, what the reader should compare first, and how to think about natural versus lab-grown at a high level. No specific prices or dates.

quality_overview_static.instruction: Summarise how to think about diamond quality (cut, colour, clarity) when browsing prices across shapes and carat weights. Educational, trust-building tone — not a sales pitch.

type_comparison.instruction: Orient first-time visitors to the difference between browsing natural versus lab-grown diamond prices on this site. Explain what each hub shows and how to choose which type to explore first — practical, non-salesy. No price figures or dates.

shape_preview.instruction: Explain how diamond shape changes the look of a stone at the same carat weight and why buyers compare shapes before locking a budget. Encourage using the linked shape and carat pages from this hub. No specific prices or market commentary.

all_shapes.intro_text:
The current price for a {carat} carat {type_label} diamond in {country_name} is {currency_symbol}{current_price:,.0f}, calculated from {num_diamonds:,} diamonds that match this carat weight and type in our database{intro_change_clause}.
Prices for these diamonds range from {currency_symbol}{min_price:,.0f} to {currency_symbol}{max_price:,.0f}, depending on factors such as the diamond shape, color, and clarity.
all_shapes.shape_analysis:
The most expensive shape for {carat} carat {type_label} diamonds is {most_expensive_shape} at {currency_symbol}{most_expensive_price:,.0f}; the least expensive is {least_expensive_shape} at {currency_symbol}{least_expensive_price:,.0f} — a {currency_symbol}{price_difference:,.0f} gap ({price_difference_pct:.1f}%).
We track {total_sample_size:,} {type} stones across {shape_count} shapes at this weight; the weighted median is {currency_symbol}{weighted_median_price:,.0f}.

diamond_type.intro: {type_title} diamond prices in {country_name} span {carat_count} carat weights in our index. The most searched weight is {most_popular_carat} carat, with a weighted median of {currency_symbol}{weighted_median_price:,.0f} across {total_sample_size:,} tracked stones.

top_level.intro: Diamond prices in {country_name} reflect broad market activity across natural and lab-grown stones. Natural diamonds average {currency_symbol}{natural_weighted_avg:,.0f} while lab-grown average {currency_symbol}{lab_weighted_avg:,.0f} in our latest snapshot.
top_level.market_size: Our index tracks {total_diamonds_tracked:,} diamonds across {natural_combo_count} natural and {lab_combo_count} lab-grown shape and carat combinations.

target_audience.primary: Diamond enthusiasts seeking objective quality metrics
target_audience.secondary: Retailers and labs evaluating the scoring API (B2B)

writing_style.editorial_stance: The reader has chosen a shape. The Sparkle Score system exists to help them find the highest-performing specimen within that shape — not to question the choice itself. When a shape scores differently to others by design (e.g. step cuts have lower brilliance scores than brilliant cuts), this is a characteristic to understand and work with, not a reason to reconsider. Any mention of what scores poorly must be immediately followed by what scores well at that shape or carat weight, and what to look for to achieve a high score. The tone is confident and empowering: the scoring system is a tool that works in the buyer's favour.
vocabulary_preference: scores highest when proportions fall within
cut quality at this carat weight
what drives the score here is
to achieve a high score on this shape
the ideal proportion window for this shape
this is where the score separates strong stones from average ones

ideal_proportions_static.instruction: The proportion ranges and cut characteristics that produce the strongest light performance in {carat}-carat {shape} diamonds. Describe the optimal depth percentage, table percentage, and length-to-width ratio (where relevant for this shape), and explain in plain terms why these proportions affect brilliance and fire. Do not reference proprietary scoring methodology or live scores — focus only on the measurable physical characteristics that separate high-performing stones from average ones at this shape and size. Close with what a buyer should look for to find a well-proportioned stone.

faq_static.instruction: Write for a buyer who cares about measurable optical performance. Generate questions specific to the proportion and cut characteristics that determine how a {carat}-carat {shape} diamond actually performs — not vague quality questions, but questions about the physical measurements that separate a high-performing stone from an average one at this shape and size. Examples: "What depth percentage range gives the best light return in a {carat}-carat {shape}?", "Does cut grade affect sparkle more than clarity in a {carat}-carat {shape}?", "What length-to-width ratio looks best for a {shape} at {carat} carats?". Answers should be technical but accessible — explain what the numbers mean for the buyer visually, not just what they are. At least one question must address a proportion trade-off specific to this shape at {carat} carats (e.g. depth vs face-up size, table vs fire). Where relevant, use value/worth phrasing (diamond score, how much are diamonds worth). No calendar years or today in static copy.

query_phrase_emphasis: how much is a {carat}-carat diamond worth

sparkle_score_system.description: The Sparkle Score is an objective measure of diamond quality

faceup_size_score.description: Visual size relative to carat weight

graph_style.meta_description_format: SparkleScore value for {carat} carat {shape} {type} diamonds in {country} — how {sample_size} stones score on price, cut, and worth. Updated {analysis_date}.

comparison.note: Pricing comparisons live on Diamond Price Exact and Ringspo, not SparkleScore.

entitlements.review_notes:
Draft v1 — standalone sites (Ringspo, DPG, DA, BDI, DHUK) may differ for
content-uniqueness (see docs/architecture/content-uniqueness-audit.md).
Translation / network families (Loupe, Diamond Price Exact, carat) MUST share
identical pipeline_artefacts and wp_widgets keys (and presentation) at each page
level — run scripts/sync_entitlements_families.py after family template changes.

Shape hub presentations (shapes_at_carat widget) — standalone sites differ by design:
shape_cards = Ringspo, Diamond Advisors, BDI; bar_chart_links = Loupe + DPE families;
table_ranked = DPG, Diamond Hunt UK; carat network = table_ranked on top-level only
(no all-shapes page — carat is fixed per domain).

Pipeline lists use artefact_id keys from config/artefacts.yaml. WP widgets
are presentation-only; a widget may be on while its artefact is still planned
(placeholder) or legacy path.

LAUNCH: All sites ship with market INDEX / pricing content only (Product 1).
consumer_advisory profiles below are Phase 8 POLICY — not live at launch.
Widgets like individual_listings are planned for Phase 8; plugin must not
render them until consumer_features_live in country_markets.yaml.

DECISION 2026-06-14 — NO CROSS-SITE PRICING COMPARISON. The cross_site_comparison
widget is NOT proceeding (network-graph exposure + price-contradiction risk; see
docs/architecture/content-uniqueness-audit.md § 0 and PRD-005 Epic CP56 ON HOLD).
All per-site cross_site_comparison values below are now set to `disabled`. The
plugin MUST NOT render this widget. Internal same-domain links (Ringspo
size<->price) are separate and allowed (PRD-015 Decision 5).
FUTURE (uncommitted): comparison MAY be revisited for a small number of pages using
THIRD-PARTY benchmarks (e.g. caratradar.com, stonealgo.com — sites that publish
price graphs), NOT reciprocal links between our own owned sites.

cross_site_comparison.live_cards: Full comparison cards (Ringspo-style) — ON HOLD, do not render
cross_site_comparison.placeholder: Section present; awaiting C5.5 + template wiring — ON HOLD
cross_site_comparison.inline_text: Woven into body copy (Diamond Advisors voice) — ON HOLD, do not render
cross_site_comparison.disabled: Not shown (target state for all sites once flags are flipped)

shapes_at_carat.shape_cards: Linked shape cards grid — crawlable anchors to each shape URL (Ringspo, DA, BDI)
shapes_at_carat.bar_chart_links: Bar chart plus linked HTML table beneath (Loupe / DPE pattern)
shapes_at_carat.bar_chart_static: Bar chart only — no shape deep links (legacy; avoid on new sites)
shapes_at_carat.table_ranked: Sortable ranked table; rows link to shape URLs (DPG, DHUK, carat network)

carat_ladder.table_pct: Table: carat weights with % change in weight and % change in price
carat_ladder.cross_domain_links: Links to sister carat domains (carat-site network only)

core_five.description: Phase 8 policy — US, UK, AU, CA, NZ on multi-country sites (not live until consumer_features_live). Other countries on the same site stay index-only.

single_us.description: Single-country US domain (Diamond Price Exact family).

single_uk.description: Single-country UK domain (Diamond Price Exact family).

single_au.description: Single-country AU domain (Diamond Price Exact family).

none.description: No consumer advisory — index/scoring/calculator/data-source only. Loupe network, SparkleScore, non-core DPE locales until market enabled.

ringspo.differentiation: Flagship expert guide: distribution snapshot (no time-series chart — Loupe owns history), color/clarity grid, buyer-side editorial; shape hub = linked cards on all-shapes pages; no carat ladder on shape pages.

diamondpriceguru.differentiation: Value-hunter: latest-only (no price-graph file), percentile callout, price alert CTA, carat ladder table on shape pages; nat/lab cards at top level.

diamondadvisors.differentiation: Premium advisor: latest-only, shape hub = linked cards on all-shapes pages, inline comparison on shape pages, color/clarity grid, carat ladder with % columns.

sparklescore.differentiation: No market-index pricing pipeline. Score gauge and editorial only — do not generate C3/C5/C5.5 pricing artefacts or price graphs.

betterdiamondinitiative.differentiation: Lab-grown only: no diamond-type level; environmental narrative widgets; latest-only; live cross-site comparison; shapes-at-carat for lab paths only.

diamondhuntuk.differentiation: UK-only paths; latest-only; British voice; carat ladder on shape pages; shape hub = ranked table on all-shapes pages; no nat/lab toggle (both types, UK paths).

modernjeweler.differentiation: US anchor Loupe site: daily time-series, shapes-at-carat bar WITH links to shape pages (L5 pattern), exit-intent, legacy individual-content path.

jewellerymonthly.differentiation: UK Loupe: time-series + shapes-at-carat bar with links; no color/clarity until opted in; comparison placeholder.

jewellerysingapore.differentiation: SG market Loupe: time-series + shapes bar; narrower widget set than modernjeweler.

jewellerycanada.differentiation: Canada Loupe: time-series + shapes bar; mid-content ad slot only on shape pages.

jewelleryaustralia.differentiation: AU Loupe: same core as SG; shapes-at-carat links required for hierarchy navigation.

jewelleryindia.differentiation: India Loupe: distribution + summary only on shape; defer time-series chart in WP until in-hi assets planned.

jewelleryireland.differentiation: Ireland Loupe: full time-series + linked shapes bar; lighter ad layout.

jewellerynz.differentiation: NZ Loupe: shapes-at-carat bar without time-series widget (pipeline still writes TS for SEO HTML).

jewellerysouthafrica.differentiation: ZA Loupe: distribution + shapes bar; comparison placeholder only.

1caratdiamondring.differentiation: Entry carat: ring-cost breakdown + setting guide; carat ladder via sister-site links (not same-site multi-carat); weekly time-series policy.

2caratdiamondring.differentiation: 2ct focus: ring-cost + size-comparison visual; cross-domain carat links.

3caratdiamondring.differentiation: 3ct: distribution + TS; setting guide only (no ring-cost table).

4caratdiamondring.differentiation: 4ct: mid-tier carat site; comparison placeholder; header ad.

5caratdiamondring.differentiation: 5ct: ring-cost breakdown returns; compact carat ladder strip in WP.

6caratdiamondring.differentiation: 6ct: distribution-only emphasis in hero; TS in secondary tab (WP).

7caratdiamondring.differentiation: 7ct: standard carat bundle; no optional widgets beyond core graphs.

8caratdiamondring.differentiation: 8ct: size-comparison + setting guide; no cross-site comparison.

10caratdiamondring.differentiation: 10ct: high-carat; ring-cost + cross-domain ladder; TS weekly.

12caratdiamondring.differentiation: 12ct: ultra-high carat; size-comparison only; no templated copy until C5.8.

diamondpriceuk.differentiation: UK English launch domain; full shape stack; TS via source; no color/clarity.

diamondpriceorg.differentiation: US English; same artefacts as UK; different graph labels via locale.

diamondpriceau.differentiation: AU English; carat_ladder compact_strip on shape pages.

diamondpricein.differentiation: India English; shapes bar static links until in-hi routing live.

diamondpriceza.differentiation: South Africa English; distribution-forward; no all-shapes until CP92.

prixdiamantfr.differentiation: FR locale; translated slugs (CP25); shapes-at-carat bar; no carat ladder v1.

diamantpreisde.differentiation: DE locale; same pipeline as FR; table-style shapes widget in WP.

prezzodiamantiit.differentiation: IT locale; minimal widget set for launch.

diamond_price_exact.differentiation: Canonical DB source only — C3/C4 run; no public S3 pricing pages.

diamondpricecalc.differentiation: Calculator UI consumes source data — no C5 market-index uploads.

always.description: No gate — always eligible for every site that has the page.

market_index_pipeline.description: Site runs the market-index pricing pipeline (C3/C4/C5/C5.5).

time_series.description: Site writes slim time-series rows, so a price history chart can exist.
time_series.authoritative_source: config/data_retention.yaml via shared/ops/time_series_helpers.py::site_has_time_series (resolved on source_site_id where set)

color_clarity.description: Site opts in to the color/clarity grid.

site_entitlement.description: Per-site product decision — artefact listed under the matching page level in site_content_entitlements.yaml.

distribution_html.description: Standalone distribution (histogram) chart — crawlable HTML page with SEO metadata.
distribution_html.runtime_note: Only written when the fat table has sample_size > 0 for the combo.

distribution_json.description: Plotly payload for the price-distribution histogram — rendered inline on the WP page (no iframe).
distribution_json.runtime_note: Only written when the fat table has sample_size > 0 for the combo.

market_index_stats_json.description: Distribution summary stats — percentiles, IQR, sample size, methodology notes.
market_index_stats_json.runtime_note: Only written when the fat table has sample_size > 0 for the combo.

market_index_bins_json.description: Histogram bucket edges and counts — raw bin data used to draw the distribution chart.
market_index_bins_json.runtime_note: Only written when the fat table has sample_size > 0 for the combo.

price_graph_html.description: Standalone price history (time-series) chart — crawlable HTML page with SEO metadata.
price_graph_html.runtime_note: Also requires >= 7 historical slim rows (MIN_TS_ROWS) at run time.

price_graph_json.description: Plotly payload for the price-over-time chart — rendered inline on the WP page.
price_graph_json.runtime_note: Also requires >= 7 historical slim rows (MIN_TS_ROWS) at run time.

og_preview_png.description: 1200×630 PNG chart preview for Open Graph / social sharing (hero distribution or time-series chart).
og_preview_png.runtime_note: Written when distribution or time-series chart is generated; requires kaleido at build time.

summary_data_json.description: Unified page stats — medians, ranges, headline numbers and metadata for the shape page.

comparison_data_json.description: Cross-site / peer price comparison payload. ON HOLD / NOT PROCEEDING (2026-06-14): network-graph exposure + price-contradiction risk. Renderer must not consume this on pricing pages.

color_clarity_json.description: Color × clarity price grid — average price per color/clarity combination.
color_clarity_json.runtime_note: C5.7 consults should_produce; legacy R4/L4 paths remain for non-entitled rollout.

static_content_json.description: AI-generated evergreen body copy — long-form editorial sections for the page.

individual_content_json.description: Legacy daily-updated dynamic copy (pre–templated-copy era). Superseded by templated_copy_json where planned.

carat_ladder_json.description: Same shape across carat weights — table with page carat highlighted; optional weight/price % columns.
carat_ladder_json.runtime_note: Same shape across carat weights; highlight page carat; optional % weight/price columns.

carat_ladder_chart.description: Bar chart of median price by carat weight for the same shape; page carat highlighted.
carat_ladder_chart.runtime_note: Rendered above the carat ladder table on shape pages when entitled.

shapes_at_carat_chart.description: Shape-vs-shape bar chart or table at a single carat — hub page linking down to individual shape pages.
shapes_at_carat_chart.runtime_note: Bar/table comparing shapes at the page carat weight. Listed under pages.all-shapes on standard sites; pages.top-level on carat EMD sites (no all-shapes URL).

all_shapes_content_json.description: Legacy AI editorial for the all-shapes page. Planned successor — templated_copy_json.

url_registry_rows.description: Database URL registry rows (not an S3 file) — canonical page URLs for routing and comparisons.

shapes_ranking_json.description: JSON ranking of shapes at a single carat — planned replacement for shapes_at_carat_chart HTML.
shapes_ranking_json.runtime_note: JSON successor to shapes_at_carat_chart HTML; same product intent.

all_shapes_summary_json.description: Aggregate stats for the all-shapes page — headline numbers across every shape at this carat.

type_summary_json.description: Natural vs lab-grown summary for the diamond-type page — rolled-up stats for that type.

market_overview_json.description: Country- or market-level overview stats — top-level pricing landing page headline data.

market_discount_chart.description: Bar chart of lab-grown discount % vs natural by carat weight on the top-level hub.

market_trend_chart.description: Line chart of natural vs lab-grown % price change over 7/14/21 days on the top-level hub.
market_trend_chart.runtime_note: Percent change from period start; sample-weighted type averages from market index daily history.

top_tables_json.description: Top shape/carat combos by sample size — popular links on standalone pricing homepages.
top_tables_json.runtime_note: Uploaded alongside market-overview.json; not gated separately in C5.3.

templated_copy_json.description: Daily templated editorial copy (non-AI) — structured sections filled from market stats.

size_summary_json.description: Per shape×carat size stats — mean/median/percentiles for dimensions, L/W ratio, face-up area (mm²) and depth %, plus n, source (real/computed) and methodology.

size_distribution_json.description: Plotly payload for the size spread/distribution chart — rendered inline on the size page (no iframe).

size_distribution_html.description: Standalone crawlable size-distribution chart — HTML page with SEO metadata + JSON-LD.

size_chart_html.description: Standalone diamond-size page (individual / hub) — crawlable HTML with SEO metadata + Dataset/Table/BreadcrumbList/FAQPage JSON-LD.

shape_outline_svg.description: True-mm diamond shape outline (recolourable, inline SVG) — actual-size render; reused by the CP109 simulator.

size_copy_json.description: Templated factual copy + FAQ + plain-text fallback for the size page (deterministic, non-AI).

size_sitemap_xml.description: Own XML sitemap for the /diamond-size/ tree — indexable individual / hub / curated-comparison URLs (router pages are not WP posts, so WP's native sitemap omits them).

carat_ladder.description: Table of the same shape across carat weights with the current page highlighted.

cross_site_comparison.description: ON HOLD / NOT PROCEEDING (2026-06-14). Cards/table comparing this site's price to peer sites. Dropped: network-graph exposure + price-contradiction risk. Do not render regardless of entitlement value. See content-uniqueness-audit.md § 0, PRD-005 CP56.

distribution_chart.description: Inline Plotly histogram of the price distribution for this shape/carat.

time_series_chart.description: Inline Plotly line chart of price history over time.

shapes_at_carat.description: Shape hub linking down to shape pages. Presentations: shape_cards (Ringspo, DA, BDI), bar_chart_links (Loupe, DPE — chart plus linked table), table_ranked (DPG, DHUK, carat). Card markup/formatting is a WP concern — not defined here.

nat_lab_toggle.description: Natural vs lab-grown type switcher on aggregate pages.

nat_lab_type_cards.description: Side-by-side natural vs lab summary cards on the diamond-type page.

individual_listings.description: Individual diamond inventory cards (Phase 8 consumer advisory — not at launch).

price_percentile_callout.description: Callout showing where the reader's budget sits on the distribution.

price_alert_cta.description: Email signup CTA for price-drop alerts (Phase 8 when consumer advisory live).

sparkle_score_gauge.description: SparkleScore gauge visualisation (SparkleScore authority site only).

shape_comparison_table.description: Tabular shape comparison (Diamond Advisors layout variant).

ring_cost_breakdown.description: Ring cost breakdown calculator widget (carat sites).

setting_guide.description: Setting / ring style guide section (carat sites).

size_distribution_chart.description: Inline size spread/distribution chart for a shape×carat. Per-site presentation (PRD-005 Decision 3) over the SAME data: distribution (full bell-curve — Ringspo), range_band (median + p10–p90), avg_plusminus (±% from percentiles), single_size (one standard size). Size presentation is a base layer only — never the sole differentiator between two sites.

size_table.description: Sortable mm↔carat size table with our extra columns (median + IQR, face-up mm², depth %, L/W range).

shape_outline.description: Inline true-mm SVG outline of the diamond shape (actual-size render).

mid_content_ad.description: Ad slot mid-page within body content.
